claim: Maximum site cover for dwelling houses in Low Density Residential zone
source_section: Section 6.2.1 — Low Density Residential Zone Code, Table 6.2.1.3.1
notes: Acceptable outcome: site cover does not exceed 50%. Used in zone-rules.ts maxSiteCover and displayed as 'Site Cover Remaining' in analysis sidebar.

claim: Maximum site cover for dwelling houses in Medium Density Residential zone
source_section: Section 6.2.3 — Medium Density Residential Zone Code
notes: 50% for standard lots. 60% for lots < 400m². zone-rules.ts uses 50% — may understate for small lots.

claim: Maximum site cover for dwelling houses in Rural Residential zone
source_section: Section 6.2.22 — Rural Residential Zone Code
notes: Used in zone-rules.ts maxSiteCover.

claim: Maximum site cover for High Density Residential zone
value: Varies (e.g. 30% for buildings >55m)
source_section: Section 6.2.4 — High Density Residential Zone Code
notes: Site cover is height-dependent — not a single flat percentage. zone-rules.ts uses 50% which is inaccurate for this zone.

claim: Character Residential zone — site cover
source_section: N/A
notes: Gold Coast does not have a 'Character Residential' zone. This is a Brisbane/other-council concept. Value is assumed 50% with no authoritative source. Needs council-specific verification if used outside Gold Coast.

claim: Maximum site cover for Low-Medium Density Residential zone
source_section: Section 6.2.2 — Low-Medium Density Residential Zone Code
notes: zone-rules.ts does not currently have a pattern for 'low-medium density' — falls through to 'medium density' match.

claim: QLD cadastre geocode_type field value PC
value: POINT OF CENTRE OF PARCELS MAKING UP A PROPERTY AND LYING WITHIN ITS BOUNDARIES (E.G. FOR L-SHAPED PROPERTY)
source_section: Appendix C — Data Dictionary (GEOCODE_TYPE_AUT table)
notes: geocode_type='PC' is the most common geocode type in QLD (2.8M of 2.8M address records). Also defined identically in the QLD Address Model at spatial-information-qld.github.io/address-model/model.html. Suggested tooltip: 'Geocode: Property Centroid — coordinate placed at the centre of the parcel boundary.'

claim: QLD cadastre tenure field value Freehold
value: Land held in Fee Simple (freehold title) which includes titles surrendered to the State of Queensland (or Crown) in terms of Section 358 of the Land Act 1994
source_document: Queensland Digital Cadastral Database (DCDB) QIF Specification
source_section: Section 7.4 — DCDB Tenure Codes (code FH)
notes: The DCDB distributes tenure as a full string ('Freehold') not the two-letter code ('FH'). QIF Sec 7.4 lists FH = FREEHOLD with this definition. Freehold is the most common tenure (2.4M of ~3.5M parcels). Suggested tooltip: 'Tenure: Freehold — land held in fee simple (freehold title).'

claim: QLD cadastre lotplan_status field value C
value: Current — the lot/plan record is the active/current cadastral record
source_authority: Queensland Department of Resources / QLD Spatial
source_document: Queensland Land Parcel Property Framework (ArcGIS REST service, Addresses layer ID 0)
source_section: Field alias 'Lot/plan status' (length 1 single-character code); sole observed value in dataset
notes: No explicit coded domain is published in the ArcGIS service or QIF spec for this field. 'C' = Current is inferred from: (1) the field alias 'Lot/plan status', (2) the single-character code pattern consistent with other DCDB status flags (S/U for surveyed indicator), and (3) 100% of 2.8M records in the QLD dataset carry the value 'C', consistent with all records being current cadastral entries. Historical lots are removed from the live dataset rather than flagged.

claim: QLD cadastre address_status field value P
value: Primary — the address record is the primary (official) address for the lot/plan
source_authority: Queensland Department of Resources / QLD PLSplus service
source_document: Queensland Property Location Service Plus (PLSplus) QG API Specification
source_section: PLSplus swagger-v3.yml — ValidateLotPlan operation description: 'Addresses shown are of a type known as Primary. A Primary address may have associated Aliases.'
notes: address_status='P' = Primary (2,729,876 records). address_status='A' = Alias/Alternative (95,294 records — secondary or informal address names for the same lot/plan). The ArcGIS labeling filter 'address_status = P' confirms only primary addresses are shown in the default map view. Suggested tooltip: 'Address status: Primary — this is the official address for this property.'

claim: QLD cadastre address_standard field value UK
value: Unknown — the addressing standard applied to this address record is not recorded
source_authority: Queensland Department of Resources / QLD Spatial (empirical — no published coded domain found)
source_document: Queensland Land Parcel Property Framework (ArcGIS REST service, Addresses layer ID 0) + local QAMF dataset analysis
source_section: Field 'address_standard' (alias: Address standard, length 4, domain: null per ArcGIS service). Full domain observed empirically: UK=Unknown (2,763,271 records, 97%), NS=Non-Standard (52,598), UR=Urban/AS4819 (6,557), RU=Rural/AS4819 (2,565), WA=Water Address (179). UR/RU verified against address samples: UR=inner-city Brisbane (460 Ann St Brisbane City), RU=rural roads (289 St Helens Road Yarranlea). NS=outback addresses without street numbers (Mcauliffe Street Aramac). WA=waterway addresses (Daintree River Upper Daintree).
notes: No ArcGIS coded domain is published (domain:null). No definition found in DCDB QIF spec, GNAF appendices, or PLSplus spec. UK meaning 'Unknown' is inferred from: (1) it is overwhelmingly the dominant value (97% of 2.8M records), consistent with a default/fallback for unclassified legacy data; (2) the two-letter abbreviation pattern matches UR=Urban, RU=Rural, NS=Non-Standard, WA=Water — making UK=Unknown the only coherent expansion; (3) AS/NZS 4819:2011 defines urban/rural addressing standards, which UR/RU directly reference. The field is NOT worth displaying in the UI — 97% of records are 'Unknown' which provides no actionable information to a homeowner.

claim: Material Change of Use (MCU) — definition under Queensland planning legislation
value: Starting a new use of premises; re-establishing an abandoned use; or materially increasing the intensity or scale of an existing use
source_section: Schedule 2 — Dictionary, entry 'material change of use'
notes: One of four categories of 'development' under s.10 Planning Act 2016. Assessed at either Code Assessment (no public notification, against applicable codes only) or Impact Assessment (public notification required, assessed against whole planning scheme). Tooltip: 'A DA type required when starting a new land use, restarting an abandoned use, or significantly increasing the scale of an existing use. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), Schedule 2.'

claim: Material Change of Use — Code Assessment definition
value: Assessed only against the applicable codes in the planning scheme; no public notification required; no third-party appeal rights
source_section: Section 45(3)
notes: Code Assessment applies when the proposed use is consistent with the zone's intended uses. Standard timeframe ~35 business days. Tooltip: 'Assessed against the applicable codes in the planning scheme only. No public notification required and neighbours cannot appeal.'

claim: Material Change of Use — Impact Assessment definition
value: Assessed against the entire planning scheme and any other relevant matter; public notification required; third-party appeal rights apply
source_section: Section 45(5)
notes: Impact Assessment applies when the proposed use is not consistent with the zone's intended uses. Requires minimum 15 business day public notification period during which submissions can be made. Tooltip: 'Assessed against the entire planning scheme. Public notification required and submitters can appeal the decision.'

claim: Reconfiguring a Lot (ROL) — definition under Queensland planning legislation
value: Creating lots by subdivision; amalgamating lots; rearranging boundaries; dividing land by agreement; or creating an access easement from a constructed road
source_section: Schedule 2 — Dictionary, entry 'reconfiguring a lot'
notes: One of four categories of 'development' under s.10. Key subtypes in Gold Coast DA data: 1-into-2 subdivision (58 DAs), boundary realignment (26), access easement (6), 1-into-3 (5). Tooltip: 'Covers subdividing land into new lots, combining lots, realigning boundaries, or creating a new access easement. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), Schedule 2.'

claim: Operational Works (OPW) — definition under Queensland planning legislation
value: Work, other than building work or plumbing or drainage work, in/on/over/under premises that materially affects premises or their use
source_section: Schedule 2 — Dictionary, entry 'operational work'
notes: One of four categories of 'development' under s.10. Broad catch-all for physical works not covered by building or plumbing approvals. Gold Coast subtypes: private tree works (596 DAs), landscaping (409), civil/ground level changes (250), development tree works (143), electrical reticulation (132). Tooltip: 'Physical changes to land that aren't building or plumbing work — earthworks, tree clearing, driveways, landscaping, and infrastructure. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), Schedule 2.'

claim: OPW Vehicle Access Works / Vehicle Crossover (VXO) — Gold Coast DA subtype
value: Operational works application for a non-standard vehicular crossing (driveway) from a property to a public road
source_document: Gold Coast City Plan — Operational Works (Vehicle Crossings)
source_section: Operational Works — Driveways/vehicular crossings
notes: VXO = Vehicular Crossing. Non-standard crossovers require DA approval (standard crossovers may be accepted development). 542 VXO DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'A development application for a non-standard driveway or vehicular crossing connecting a property to a public road.'

claim: Prescribed Tidal Works — definition under Queensland coastal legislation
value: Works consisting of tidal works (construction/demolition of structures in tidal areas: jetties, pontoons, seawalls, boat ramps, wharves, marinas) or works integrally including tidal works
source_section: Section 15 and Schedule 4 (Dictionary); parent definition in Coastal Protection and Management Act 1995 (Qld)
notes: Assessed by local government when located in a tidal area within an LGA boundary. Must comply with Schedule 3 design code. Exclusions: State-managed boat harbours, port operations, navigational channels. 282 pontoon DAs and 3 seawall DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'Works built or demolished in tidal water — jetties, pontoons, seawalls, boat ramps — assessed under the Coastal Protection and Management Regulation 2017.'

claim: Minor Change to a development approval — definition under Queensland planning legislation
value: A change to a development approval that would not result in substantially different development
source_section: Planning Act 2016 s.78 (application), s.81 (assessment); DA Rules Schedule 1 (substantially different development test)
notes: Minor change = does not involve a new use, new land parcel, new referral agency, prohibited development, or shift from code to impact assessment. No public notification required. 20 business day assessment timeframe. 612 minor change applications in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'A modest modification to an existing development approval that does not result in substantially different development. No public notification required. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), s.78.'

claim: Extension of Approval (currency period) — Queensland planning legislation
value: Application to extend the period within which a development approval must be acted on before it lapses
source_section: Section 85 (currency period), Section 86 (extension application), Section 87 (decision)
notes: Default currency periods: MCU = 6 years, ROL = 4 years, Building/OPW = 2 years. Must be lodged before approval lapses. Owner consent required. 79 extension DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'An application to extend the period within which a development approval must be acted on before it lapses. Must be lodged before expiry. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), s.86.'

claim: Combined Application — definition under Queensland planning legislation
value: A single development application covering two or more types of development (e.g. MCU + operational works) assessed under one process
source_section: Section 51 — Making development applications
notes: Not separately defined in Schedule 2 Dictionary — a procedural concept arising from s.51's permission to include multiple development types in one application. Where multiple assessment managers apply, DA Rules designate the principal assessment manager. 29 combined DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'A single DA covering two or more development types (e.g. MCU + operational works) assessed together in one process. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), s.51.'

claim: Priority Development Area (PDA) — definition under Queensland legislation
value: A declared area where the State Government controls planning to fast-track economic development; overrides local council planning scheme
source_section: Section 37 (declaration), Section 38 (interim land use plan), Section 4 (main purpose)
jurisdiction: QLD — Gold Coast (Robina PDA, Southport PDA)
notes: PDAs like Robina Town Centre PDA and Southport PDA have their own development schemes administered by Economic Development Queensland (EDQ) rather than council. Development in PDAs is assessed against the PDA development scheme, not the Gold Coast City Plan. Tooltip: 'A State-declared area where the State Government controls planning instead of council, to fast-track economic development. PDAs have their own development scheme. Economic Development Act 2012 (Qld), s.37.'

claim: Robina Final Development Approval (Robina FDA) — Gold Coast DA type
value: A Final Development Approval issued under the Robina Central Business District Development Agreement (a pre-IPA legacy planning instrument for Robina Town Centre)
source_authority: City of Gold Coast / Economic Development Queensland
source_document: Robina Central Business District Planning Agreement (legacy instrument, pre-IPA)
source_section: Robina Town Centre development approval framework
notes: A legacy approval type specific to the Robina Town Centre PDA. 20 Robina FDA applications in Gold Coast dataset. These are final approvals under the historic Robina Central Business District development agreement, distinct from the standard Planning Act 2016 approval pathway. Tooltip: 'A Final Development Approval for the Robina Town Centre, assessed under the historic Robina CBD development agreement rather than the standard planning scheme.'

claim: Express Development Application — Gold Coast fast-track DA process
value: A council-administered fast-track process for low-complexity, code-assessable DAs decided within 10–15 business days
source_document: Gold Coast City Council — Express Development Applications
source_section: Express DA program description and eligibility criteria
notes: Not a separate legislative category — an administrative program offered at council discretion. Eligible types: dual occupancy, lot reconfiguration (up to 5 lots), multiple dwelling (3–10 units), low-impact industry, warehouse, food/drink outlet, healthcare, office, shop. Must be code assessable. 3 Express DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'A fast-track council process for simple, code-assessable DAs decided within 10–15 business days instead of the standard 45.'

claim: Superseded Planning Scheme — Queensland planning legislation
value: Application assessed under the previous planning scheme rather than the current one; available within 1 year of a planning change
source_section: Section 29 (superseded planning scheme requests)
notes: Must apply within 1 year of the planning scheme change. If agreed, applicant has 6 months to lodge. Useful when new planning rules would make development harder to approve. 1 superseded planning scheme DA in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'Lets you apply to have your DA assessed under the previous planning scheme if it was recently replaced or amended. Must apply within 1 year of the change. Planning Act 2016 (Qld), s.29.'

claim: Temporary Ground Anchors — Queensland planning DA type
value: Operational works application for temporary ground anchors used during construction (e.g. tie-back anchors for retaining walls or excavation support)
source_document: Gold Coast City Council — Development application types
source_section: Operational Works — Temporary Ground Anchors
notes: A specific OPW sub-type for temporary anchors drilled through or under neighbouring properties during construction. Typically requires neighbour consent and approval because the anchor passes beyond the applicant's property boundary. 9 Temporary Ground Anchor DAs in Gold Coast dataset. Tooltip: 'A development application for temporary anchors used during construction (e.g. tie-back anchors for excavation support), typically where anchors extend under neighbouring land.'
